sections.text: Welcome back. Algebra One, Module Five — multi-step and literal equations. Last time we did one-step and two-step. Today we level up. Same rules, more moves.

Module Four taught you how to solve x plus five equals twelve. Easy. Today we tackle three x plus seven equals two x plus twelve. Variables on both sides. We tackle five times the quantity x minus two equals fifteen — parentheses that need distributing. We tackle x over four plus x over six equals five — fractions. And we tackle solving formulas for a chosen variable. Sounds harder. Same rules underneath.

Four moves to add. One — distribute parentheses first. Two — combine like terms. Three — get the variable on one side only. Four — clear fractions by multiplying through. Then a fifth thing — literal equations, where the answer isn't a number, it's a rearranged formula.

Distribute first. When you see something multiplying parentheses, fix that before doing anything else. Five times the quantity x minus two equals fifteen. Distribute the five — five x minus ten equals fifteen. Now it's just a two-step equation. Add ten, get five x equals twenty-five. Divide by five, x equals five. Done.

Combine like terms. Like terms are terms with the same variable to the same power. Three x and five x are like terms. Three x and three x squared are NOT like terms. Same letter doesn't mean like. Combining like terms shrinks the equation before you start solving. Two x plus seven plus three x minus four equals fifteen. Combine — five x plus three equals fifteen. Now it's a two-step. Subtract three, divide by five. x equals two point four.

Variables on both sides. Three x plus seven equals two x plus twelve. The trick — pick a side, move the other variable over there. Subtract two x from both sides. Three x minus two x is x. So x plus seven equals twelve. Subtract seven. x equals five. Pro tip — move the smaller coefficient. Avoids negatives.
Your turn. Solve four x minus six equals two x plus eight. Pause and work it out. Move the smaller variable side first.

Time. Subtract two x from both sides. Two x minus six equals eight. Add six. Two x equals fourteen. Divide by two. x equals seven. Plug it back to check — four times seven minus six is twenty-two. Two times seven plus eight is twenty-two. Match.

Fractions. The shortcut is to clear them first. Find the least common denominator of all the fractions in the equation, then multiply every term by that denominator. Fractions vanish, and you're left with a normal equation. Example coming.

Solve x over two plus x over three equals five. The denominators are two and three. LCD is six. Multiply every term by six. Six times x over two is three x. Six times x over three is two x. Six times five is thirty. So three x plus two x equals thirty. Five x equals thirty. x equals six. Notice — never carried a fraction past the first move.

Literal equations. The new idea. Up till now, you've solved for x and the answer was a number. With a literal equation, the answer is itself an equation — a formula rearranged for a chosen variable. Same rules, just symbols all the way down.

Example. Area of a rectangle is A equals l times w. Solve for w. Treat A and l as numbers. We want w alone. Divide both sides by l. w equals A over l. Done. We just rearranged the formula. Same algebra rules — divide both sides — applied to letters instead of numbers.

One more. The ideal gas law — P V equals n R T. Solve for T. Divide both sides by n R. T equals P V over n R. Two seconds. The intimidation factor of physics formulas mostly disappears once you realize they're literal equations and you can rearrange them with Module Four math.
Last try-it. Solve the formula d equals r times t for r. Pause and rearrange. Treat d and t as numbers.
Welcome back. d equals r t. Divide both sides by t. r equals d over t. That's exactly the equation 'rate equals distance over time' that you've seen in physics. You just derived it from d equals r t.

Recap. Distribute parentheses first. Combine like terms. Get the variable on one side. Clear fractions by multiplying by the LCD. Literal equations are just letter-rearranging. Every move uses the same rules from Module Four — whatever you do to one side, do to the other. The only difference is now there's more moves to chain together.

To master this, read OpenStax Chapters two point four and two point five. Run the Khan Academy 'variables on both sides' practice. Your dashboard assignment is ten multi-step equations plus five literal equations to rearrange. The literal ones look weird at first; they get easy after three. See you in Module Six — inequalities and interval notation, where one tiny detail breaks everything you just learned.
